preamble: The shift from the alveolar stop [t] to the velar fricative [x] is a rare form of extreme lenition and retraction. It typically occurs in syllable-coda positions in dialects with high degrees of spirantization, where the sound moves from the front of the mouth to the back-of-the-throat 'default' friction position.

examples.note: Scouse lenition: coda stops are realized as fricatives, with /t/ occasionally retracting toward velar or glottal friction.
In complex consonant mutation environments, dental stops can lenite and retract into velar fricatives.

sources: Thurneysen: A Grammar of Old Irish
